coredata.dc:title: A “turn-on” fluorescent sensor for Pb2+ detection based on nitrogen doping carbon dots

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Optical properties of the fabricated N-CDs
Fluorescence response of the fabricated N-CDs to Pb2+

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM and (b) HRTEM images of N-CDs. (c) The particle size distribution of N-CDs. (d) XRD patterns of N-CDs and (e) Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) comparison between precursor dopamine and N-CDs. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis of N-CDs: (f) survey spectrum, high resolution XPS spectra of (g) C 1s, (h) O 1s, and (i) N 1s orbitals.

(a) Fluorescence responses and (b) linear relationship of enhancement ratio (F/F0) of N-CDs versus Pb2+ at different concentrations. (c) Fluorescence responses and (d) F/F0 comparison of N-CDs under different heavy metal ions. (e) FTIR spectra comparison and (f) fluorescence decay profiles of N-CDs with/without Pb2+ addition (excitation wavelength = 440 nm; ionic concentration = 50 μM).
Schematic illustration of Pb2+ induced fluorescence enhancement of N-CDs by an inhibited PET process.

head.ce:title: A “turn-on” fluorescent sensor for Pb2+ detection based on nitrogen doping carbon dots

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
A “turn-on” fluorescence sensing platform towards Pb2+ with high selectivity and sensitivity was established by nitrogen-doped carbon dots. A suppressed photo-induced electron transfer (PET) was proposed for the sensing mechanism. This work can advance the development of nanomaterial-based sensors for in-situ detection of environmental samples.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Due to the severe toxicity of heavy metal ions, human health and eco-system are threatened even at ultra-low concentrations [1]. Specially, Pb2+ arouses more significant concern as its bio-accumulation seriously affects brain development, reproductive systems, and cardiovascular function, consequently resulting in an irreversible damage. According to World Health Organization (WHO), the maximum allowable Pb2+ concentration on the surface water should be < 0.01 mg/L. Thus, considering such deleterious and carcinogenic effects, the Pb2+ amount should be monitored to guarantee the water environment safety. Conventional methods always need complicate pretreatment, expensive instrument, and technical operators, greatly limiting their broad applications [2]. In contrast, nano-materials based platform is becoming an emerging trend in environmental analysis and detection. Particularly, quantum dots (QDs) showcase unique optical properties with the sizes decreased, significantly promoting their prospective in imaging and sensing [3].

Different from semiconductor based QDs always containing heavy metal ingredients, carbon dots (CDs) are more competitive candidates due to their lower bio-toxicity, chemical inertness, and inexpensive cost [4]. Moreover, the properties of CDs can be dramatically improved and regulated through content and surface engineering [5,6]. Among them, nitrogen doping in CDs (N-CDs) is the most efficacious way, which introduces impurity energy level and electron-rich center, obviously improving the fluorescent sensing properties towards heavy metal ions [7]. However, it is still challenging to facilely introduce nitrogen dopant in CDs with a specific affinity towards Pb2+. Therefore, more green and sustainable approaches for specific N-CDs are necessary and their sensing performances should be explored as well.

Herein, a neutralization heat strategy was creatively employed to prepare unique N-CDs from dopamine precursor within an ultrashort time (∼2 min). The physiochemical and optical properties of N-CDs were comprehensively discovered through a series of characterizations. The established fluorescence sensing platform by N-CDs showed a unique “turn-on” response in the presence of Pb2+ ions with a LOD up to 0.92 μM. To explain the sensing mechanism, a PET inhibition process was proposed for the enhanced fluorescence of N-CDs with Pb2+ introduced, which was further demonstrated by DFT study and experimental evidence. This work solidifies the promising applications of carbonaceous materials in environmental sensing.

A fast neutralization heat method was employed here to synthesize N-CDs (Scheme S1). With intensive heat released by neutralization reaction between ethylenediamine and phosphoric acid, dopamine precursor could be in situ polymerized and quickly transformed into N-CDs (∼2 min). Transmission electron microscope (TEM) images revealed that the size dimensions of N-CDs mostly lied in 2–8 nm with the average to be ∼6.86 nm (Fig. 1 a and c). The high-resolution TEM (HR-TEM) image (Fig. 1b) indicated a graphite phase in N-CDs with a lattice space of 0.235 nm, in accordance with the (112) crystal lattice [8]. Moreover, X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of N-CDs indicated a broad diffraction peak at 2θ = 23o (Fig. 1d), which revealed a graphitic structure and demonstrated the HR-TEM results.

FTIR technology can give more information about the surface groups in N-CDs. The differences between dopamine precursor and N-CDs were compared to find the surface of surface group evolution after the synthesis process (Fig. 1e). Compared with intricate peaks in dopamine, N-CDs showcased suppressed intensity but remained the similar outline. Among them, the peaks at 3435 and 1283 cm−1 of N-CDs were ascribed to the C–OH stretching and –OH bending vibrations. The peaks at 1613 and 1520 cm−1 were attributed to the bending vibration of N–H. Besides, the peaks at 1170, 1332 and 1495 cm−1 correspond to the stretching vibrations of C–N, the phenolic hydroxyl group O–H, and C=C of N-CDs, respectively. This evidence indicates the obtained N-CDs from dopamine kept the main framework but assembled into a high-weight one.

Furthermore, XPS was carried out to present more details of surface chemical states of N-CDs. The XPS results (Fig. 2 f) confirmed the existence of C, O and N elements in the as-prepared N-CDs with their corresponding atom ratios as 52.53%, 29.49%, and 17.98%. Additionally, the high-resolution XPS of C 1s spectrum was deconvoluted into three peaks at 284.6, 285.8 and 288.2 eV, assigned to C–C, C–O and C=O/C=N bonds, corresponding to the carbonaceous framework of N-CDs. Three peaks at 530.4, 531.3 and 532.3 eV in the high-resolution XPS of O 1s orbital were ascribed to C=O, O–H and C–O groups. In addition, the N 1s orbital was deconvoluted peaks at 399.7 and 400.7 eV, which were assigned to pyridinic and pyrrolic N, respectively [9]. All these results collectively confirmed the abundant O and N functional groups present in N-CDs, in agreement with the FTIR results obtained.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties of the fabricated N-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: UV-vis absorption spectra (Fig. S1) of N-CDs exhibited two typical peaks at 260 and 327 nm, respectively. Among them, one band at 260 nm was ascribed to the π-π∗ transition of C=C in aromatic ring structure, and the other one at 327 nm may be relative to the n-π∗ transition [10,11]. Meanwhile, N-CDs exhibited an excitation-dependent emission with a subtle red-shift (Fig. S2). This phenomenon is probably caused by different dimensions and surface states [12]. Besides, the long emission wavelength of fluorescence (∼550 nm) might be ascribed to the existence of C=N/C=O and C–N functional groups/structures on the surface of N-CDs [13]. More significantly, pH influence on the fluore Scence of N-CDs presented an increased tendency from 3 to 6 (Fig. S3), and then decreased above 6. According to previous experience, this pH-sensitive property could be induced by the group (de)protonation of N-CDs [13]. At low pH, the surface protonation of N-CDs took place to weaken the fluorescence intensity, while at high pH, the residue dopamine/dopamine analogue in structure was oxidized into quinone structures, in favor of accepting electrons to quench the fluorescence. As a consequence, the N-CDs exhibited the highest fluorescence intensity at the pH of 6. To prove the long-term stability, the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs was also monitored under both continuous irradiation and long-term storage (Fig. S4-S5). It was observed that the fluorescence intensity decreased slightly after an exposure excitation for 120 min, revealing the superior fluorescence stability. Also, the florescent properties of N-CDs were unaffected after a long-term storage, demonstrating their durability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence response of the fabricated N-CDs to Pb2+
ce:section.ce:para: Inspired by the specific affinity of polydopamine towards Pb2+, the fluorescent sensing feasibility of N-CDs derived from dopamine precursor warrants more exploration [14]. Fig. 2a shows that the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs increased with Pb2+ concentration ranging from 0 to 50 μM. According to the fitting results, there are two linear parts separately in the ranges of 0–25 and 25–50 μM (Fig. 2b). The LOD of this sensing platform is estimated as 0.92 μM on the basis of 3σ rule. Moreover, the selectivity of the N-CDs fluorescent sensor was investigated (Fig. 2c–d), finding only Pb2+ exhibited an obvious effect on the fluorescence signal, while other metal ions showcased little interference. This could be presumably attributed to the special affinity between Pb2+ and the existing chemical groups in N-CDs. Thus, it could be deduced that the presence of various inference metal ions in the taken water specimen would not disturb the fluorescence response of N-CDs toward Pb2+, demonstrating their specific sensing capacity to ensure the analysis accuracy. Interestingly, the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs with Pb2+ was reversed back to the initial state after adding disodium ethylene diamine tetraacetate (EDTA-2Na, Fig. S6), which could be assigned to their stronger chelation towards Pb2+, thus indicating this sensing platform is more suitable to free Pb2+ detection rather than the chelated one.

From simulated molecular electrostatic potential (MEP) plots of N-CDs (Fig. S7), the possible coordination site could be located at the hydroxyl groups because of a more negative region in favor of electrophilic affinity of Pb2+. Accordingly, a fluorescence transformation of N-CDs from weak to bright could be caused by a PET inhabitation process after Pb2+ affinity. To prove the mechanism, TD-DFT (Time-dependent density functional theory) calculation of the optimized N-CDs structure was carried out as well. As shown in Table S1, N-CDs show two main types of transition whose oscillator strengths are 0.14 and 0.44 corresponding to the calculated wavelength of 362 and 335 nm, respectively. According to the oscillator strengths, the singlet electronic transition of HOMO-1 → LUMO (S 0 → S 2) would take place, agreeing with the maximum absorption wavelength of 327 nm in Fig. S1. It clearly suggested a PET process from HOMO to HOMO-1 caused the fluorescence quenching [15]. After incorporating Pb2+ into N-CDs (N-CD-Pb2+), the fluorescence was enhanced by a suppressed PET process.

Furthermore, ab initio calculation of the N-CDs-Pb2+ complex was performed to further confirm that view. It indicates singlet electronic excitation of the complex N-CDs-Pb2+ also shows two main types of transition, of which HOMO → LUMO (S 0 → S 1) transition contributes more proportion (Table S1). As shown in Fig. S8, the LUMO and HOMO states of N-CDs-Pb2+ complex mainly focused on the Pb2+ and heterocyclic ring system unit with a smaller energy gap from HOMO to LUMO. This smaller gap is in favor of electronic transition, in which PET quenching process is suppressed, leading to fluorescence enhancement [16]. In summary, the proposed mechanism is vividly illustrated in Fig. 3 .

To further demonstrate the mechanism, FTIR and fluorescence decay curves were used to find the binding sites and exhibited lifetime of N-CDs. In the FTIR spectrum (Fig. 2e), the pristine peaks assigned to the hydroxyl group in N-CDs were obviously weakened after the addition of Pb2+ in the solution. This evidence indicated hydroxyl groups are the binding sites of Pb2+, in consistence with the calculation result. Moreover, the fluorescence decay curves exhibited lifetime differences of N-CDs before and after the affinity of Pb2+ ions (Fig. 2f), which are perfectly fitted to a dual exponential function, indicative of the existence of multi-radiative modes. This also agreed with two main types of transition in the theoretical calculations. As a consequence, the average calculated lifetimes are 3.32 and 4.83 ns, respectively for N-CDs with/without Pb2+. The obtained information is in line with the fluorescence sensing results towards Pb2+, confirming the simulated speculation mentioned above.

To better present the superiority of this work, the comparison to several previous works in carbon dots synthesis and sensing applications is also conducted (Table S2), showing that our N-CDs exhibit a satisfied detection result although the effective sensing range still needs to be improved in the future work. To further investigate their practical performance, a series of recovery tests using tap water and reservoir water as samples were conducted and compared. Different amounts of Pb2+ ions were pre-added into the water samples, followed by a filter purification to remove impurities before fluorescent detection. The obtained results indicated the detection performance for these practical water samples was acceptable (Table S3), demonstrating the great potential of N-CDs based sensing platform towards Pb2+ in the real water environment.

In summary, a fast neutralization heat strategy was employed herein to synthesize N-CDs with a specific “turn-on” fluorescence behavior for Pb2+ sensing. This established sensing platform could perform a linear fluorescent response towards Pb2+ in the concentration range of 0–50 μM. More significantly, both an excellent detection sensitivity (LOD of 0.92 μM) and selectivity were achieved for Pb2+ detection. Accordingly, a suppressed PET process caused by Pb2+ was proposed for the unique “turn-on” sensing mechanism of N-CDs, which was also proved by DFT simulation and experimental analysis. This work can inspire a broad development of nano chem-sensors in the field of environmental analysis and monitoring.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare no competing interests.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by grants from the Major Program of the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21590813), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 22006039, 52100128, 22122807), the Key Project of the National Ministry of Science and Technology (No. 2016YFC0204204), the Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province (No. ZR2020QB141), the Natural Science Foundation of Shanghai (No. 21ZR1425700), and the China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (No. 2019M661412). This work is also supported by Supercomputing Center of Dalian University of Technology.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Rationally engineered nanosensors: a novel strategy for the detection of heavy metal ions in the environment
J. Hazard Mater.
